screens.prompt: Case complication: it turns out there are actually *two* transmissions occupying the same frequency band as the burst — a second, unrelated signal with a different symbol rate sits right on top of it in an ordinary spectrogram. Can cyclic features help tell them apart even though they overlap completely in ordinary frequency?
choices: No — if they occupy the same frequencies, no spectral technique can separate them
Yes — the two signals almost certainly have different cycle frequencies (different symbol rates), and the SCF separates along that extra axis
screens.reveal: Yes. Two signals occupying the exact same frequency band are typically still generated by clocks running at different rates — different symbol rates, different framing — so their SCFs peak at different cycle frequencies even where their ordinary spectra sit right on top of each other. Filtering or analyzing based on cycle frequency lets you isolate one signal's contribution from the other's, something that's structurally impossible using frequency alone. This is the same "extra axis" idea from the core-theory module, now put to direct practical use: the α dimension carries information the f dimension alone simply doesn't have.

screens.prompt: Why can two signals that fully overlap in ordinary frequency still be separated using cyclic features?

choices.text: They typically have different symbol/framing rates, so their SCFs peak at different cycle frequencies even when their ordinary spectra coincide
Cyclic features can only separate signals that don't overlap in frequency at all
choices.feedback: That's exactly the case cyclic features are useless for improving on — the interesting, useful case is when signals DO overlap in frequency but differ in cycle frequency.

choices.text: One of the signals must always be much stronger than the other
choices.feedback: Relative signal strength isn't what makes cyclic separation work — it's the difference in cycle frequency (driven by different clocking rates) that provides the separating axis, regardless of relative power.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — the cycle-frequency axis is an extra degree of freedom that ordinary frequency-domain analysis simply doesn't have access to, and it's often exactly what two co-channel signals differ in.
